hypothesis: Advanced players have distinct shot sequence signatures that could power a coaching product
venue_bias_flag: FLAGGED: All clips from single Courtana venue (apparent single pickleball facility). Cannot generalize to all skill levels or play styles.

discoveries.title: The '4.0 Fingerprint': speed_up→block→speed_up Chain Appears Exclusively in 4.0+ Players
discoveries.finding: The attack-defend-attack chain (speed_up→block→speed_up) appears 7 times in 4.0+ clips vs 2 times in 3.5-4.0 and 0 times in 3.0-3.5. This aggressive counter-attack pattern requires both the patience to block first and the athleticism to re-attack immediately. Distribution by DUPR: {'3.5-4.0': 2, 'unknown': 4, '4.0-4.5': 3}
discoveries.why_it_matters: This 3-shot sequence is a concrete, demonstrable 'advanced player signature' that directly validates the coaching product hypothesis. Players can be graded on whether they execute this pattern or mis-execute it.
discoveries.sampling_bias_flag: 4.0+ clips may be underrepresented (only 4 clips) — pattern rarity could reflect sample size not skill boundary.
discoveries.strongest_counter_argument: With only 4 clips in the 4.0+ band, the pattern could be venue-specific (one or two specific players) rather than a skill-level phenomenon.

discoveries.title: Advanced Players Use Dinks as Setup Weapons, Not Just Rallying Shots
discoveries.finding: 3.5+ DUPR players convert dinks to attacks at 4.1% rate vs 1.9% for 3.0-3.5. The advanced signature is dink→speed_up (not just longer dink rallies). This predatory patience — building pressure in the kitchen then attacking — is a quantifiable behavioral distinction.
discoveries.why_it_matters: If we can teach players to dink strategically instead of passively, that's a coaching product with measurable ROI — trackable before/after via the pipeline.
discoveries.sampling_bias_flag: All clips from single Courtana venue. Skill self-reported via visual AI estimation, not verified DUPR.
discoveries.strongest_counter_argument: The dink-to-speed-up pattern may be bimodal by clip type (selected highlights vs full match rally clips), not true skill-level difference.

discoveries.title: Speed-Up Attack Converts Only 12.5% of Time; Errors 20.8% — Riskiest Shot in Corpus
discoveries.finding: Speed-ups (aggressive kitchen attacks) succeed as outright winners only 12.5% of the time, while generating errors 20.8% of the time. The majority (66.7%) simply continue the rally. This counter-intuitive data challenges 'go aggressive at kitchen' coaching advice.
discoveries.why_it_matters: Coaching product can alert players when their speed-up selection is statistically suboptimal — a 'shot selection coach' that reviews your risk profile.
discoveries.sampling_bias_flag: Highlights are biased toward exciting rallies; speed-up success rate may be inflated vs. typical match play.
discoveries.strongest_counter_argument: Highlight-selected clips over-represent successful speed-ups because failed speed-ups (unforced errors) end rallies before the 'exciting moment' that triggers the highlight system.

discoveries.title: Kitchen Mastery Is the Best Single Predictor of DUPR Band — Linear 3.0→8.0 Ladder
discoveries.finding: Kitchen mastery rating climbs monotonically: 2.5-3.0 = 3.0, 3.0-3.5 = 5.9, 3.5-4.0 = 6.8, 4.0-4.5 = 8.0. This is the most linear skill progression indicator in the corpus — more so than court IQ, touch, or power. Every DUPR tier shows a +1 point jump in kitchen mastery.
discoveries.why_it_matters: Kitchen mastery as a single coaching 'north star metric' makes the coaching product simple to sell: 'We track your kitchen game. Every point higher = measurable DUPR improvement.'
discoveries.sampling_bias_flag: Skill levels are AI-estimated from video, not verified DUPR scores. Circular reasoning risk if model correlates visual kitchen proximity with skill.
discoveries.strongest_counter_argument: The Gemini model may be assigning DUPR estimates based on kitchen mastery observations, creating artificial correlation — the AI could be circular reasoning with itself.

discoveries.title: Higher Skill = Shorter, More Decisive Rallies — Beginner Clips Average 2x Longer
discoveries.finding: Average rally length: 2.5-3.0 DUPR = 2.0 shots, 3.0-3.5 DUPR = 13.6 shots, 3.5-4.0 DUPR = 21.6 shots, 4.0-4.5 DUPR = 8.2 shots. Advanced players close rallies faster — not with power but with strategic speed-up selection after fewer setup dinks.
confidence_caps_applied: Highlight selection bias — longer rallies more likely to be highlighted
discoveries.why_it_matters: Rally length efficiency is a measurable coaching metric. Players can track their 'decisiveness quotient' over time — a compelling retention hook for a coaching app.
discoveries.sampling_bias_flag: CRITICAL: Highlight bias heavily skews this — longer, more exciting rallies are far more likely to be captured as highlights, inflating beginner rally lengths artificially.
discoveries.strongest_counter_argument: Longer beginner rallies may entirely be a selection artifact of what Courtana's AI flags as highlights — this finding may not survive cross with full match data.
